title: The Betrothed
author: Alessandro Manzoni
description: Set in seventeenth-century Lombardy under Spanish rule, two peasant lovers are separated by a tyrannical nobleman, launching an epic that weaves plague, war, and religious faith into the founding masterpiece of Italian prose fiction.

title: The House by the Medlar Tree
author: Giovanni Verga
description: A Sicilian fishing family's slow ruin under poverty, bad luck, and the indifferent forces of society — Verga's landmark Naturalist novel, the first in his unfinished Vinti cycle, told in the collective voice of the village itself.

title: Mastro-don Gesualdo
author: Giovanni Verga
description: A self-made Sicilian builder claws his way from poverty to wealth and respectability, only to find that money cannot buy him acceptance in the class he has joined — Verga's second Vinti novel and a masterpiece of Italian realism.

title: Cavalleria Rusticana and Other Stories
author: Giovanni Verga
description: Stark, unsentimental tales of Sicilian peasant life — jealousy, honor, and sudden violence rendered in Verga's revolutionary verismo style, including the title story that inspired Mascagni's famous opera.

title: Little Novels of Sicily
author: Giovanni Verga
description: A second essential collection of Sicilian stories translated by D. H. Lawrence, capturing the harshness and dignity of rural life with the compressed, documentary power that defines Verga's mature verismo.

title: Fortunata and Jacinta
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: Two women — one a respectable bourgeois wife, the other a passionate woman of the people — are bound together through their love for the same weak, charming man in Galdós's vast panorama of Madrid society, considered the greatest Spanish novel after Don Quixote.

title: Doña Perfecta
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A liberal young engineer visits his aunt in a provincial Spanish town and finds rigid religious fanaticism and social intolerance capable of destroying everything he loves — a fierce indictment of clerical reaction in nineteenth-century Spain.

title: Marianela
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A deformed, selfless young woman guides a blind boy through the world of nature and imagination, and is shattered when a surgeon's gift of sight allows him to see — and to choose — for the first time.

title: The Spendthrifts
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A middle-class Madrid couple obsessed with keeping up appearances desperately scheme to conceal their poverty while the queen's maid-in-waiting hauls them further into ruin — sharp, comic social satire.

title: Torment
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A clergyman's former mistress tries to escape her past and marry respectably in Madrid, only to be blackmailed by a scoundrel who knows her history — a tightly plotted study of hypocrisy and social cruelty.

title: Miau
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A proud but hapless retired civil servant is consumed by his obsession with regaining his government post while his family falls apart around him, in one of Galdós's most tragicomic portraits of Madrid's declassed middle class.

title: Nazarín
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A wandering, Christ-like Madrid priest abandons all possessions and social ties to live among the poor and outcast, testing whether radical evangelical charity is possible in modern Spain.

title: Misericordia
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: An elderly servant woman secretly begs in the streets to support her impoverished mistress, sustaining their lives through compassion and invention — Galdós's most moving portrait of poverty and saintly resilience in Madrid.

title: Tristana
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A young woman trapped as the ward and mistress of a selfish older man dreams of independence and artistic self-creation, in Galdós's bleakly ironic study of female aspiration crushed by nineteenth-century Spanish society.

title: Torquemada at the Stake
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: A ruthless Madrid moneylender, faced with his beloved son's fatal illness, frantically attempts acts of charity to bargain with God — the savage, ironic first novel of Galdós's Torquemada tetralogy.

title: Torquemada on the Cross
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: The grasping moneylender Torquemada is maneuvered into marriage with an impoverished aristocratic family, beginning his improbable ascent into the Spanish upper class in the second Torquemada novel.

title: Torquemada in Purgatory
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: Now wealthy and titled, Torquemada is groomed into a respectable financier and senator by his cultured sister-in-law, even as greed corrodes whatever soul he has left.

title: Torquemada and Saint Peter
author: Benito Pérez Galdós
description: On his deathbed, the immensely rich Torquemada is fought over by priests and heirs in the final novel of Galdós's sardonic tetralogy on greed, class-climbing, and spiritual bankruptcy.

title: The Crime of Father Amaro
author: Eça de Queirós
description: A young seminary-trained priest seduces a devout young woman in a provincial Portuguese town, with fatal consequences — Portugal's first great realist novel, scandalous on publication and closely modeled on Flaubert and Zola.

title: Cousin Bazilio
author: Eça de Queirós
description: A bored Lisbon housewife embarks on an affair with her returned cousin and finds herself at the mercy of a blackmailing servant — a Naturalist dissection of bourgeois marriage and moral cowardice in the Portuguese capital.

title: The Maias
author: Eça de Queirós
description: Three generations of a wealthy Lisbon family are traced through aristocratic decline and a devastating incestuous love affair, while the novel savages the complacency of Portugal's ruling class with wit and elegance.

title: The Relic
author: Eça de Queirós
description: A hypocritical young Portuguese man travels to Jerusalem to bring back a holy relic for his pious aunt and instead brings home a scandal — a wickedly comic novel skewering religious hypocrisy and bourgeois self-deception.

title: The Illustrious House of Ramires
author: Eça de Queirós
description: A young nobleman of ancient lineage writes a chivalric romance about his heroic ancestors while behaving ignobly in the present, in Eça's late meditation on Portuguese identity, nostalgia, and the gap between myth and reality.

title: The City and the Mountains
author: Eça de Queirós
description: A wealthy Portuguese dandy devoted to Paris and modern technology is coaxed back to his ancestral estate and discovers, with gentle irony, that rural simplicity and tradition offer a richer life than all of civilization's pleasures.

title: The Tragedy of the Street of Flowers
author: Eça de Queirós
description: Written in the 1870s but withheld for a century, this passionate Lisbon love story ends in catastrophe when the lovers discover they are related — Eça's most Romantic novel, suppressed in his lifetime for its incestuous subject.

title: The Red Room
author: August Strindberg
description: A disillusioned young idealist navigates the corrupt worlds of Stockholm journalism, politics, art, and bureaucracy in Strindberg's debut novel — a satirical landmark that launched Swedish literary Modernism.

title: The People of Hemsö
author: August Strindberg
description: A lusty, ambitious farmhand arrives on a Stockholm archipelago island, marries the widow who owns it, and overreaches himself — a vivid, comic naturalist novel set in the Swedish skerry landscape Strindberg loved best.

title: Getting Married
author: August Strindberg
description: Stories anatomizing the battle of the sexes with savage wit and provocative fury, scandalizing Sweden and resulting in Strindberg's prosecution for blasphemy — a defining document of the era's gender wars.

title: A Madman's Defence
author: August Strindberg
description: An autobiographical novel written in French detailing Strindberg's tortured first marriage with obsessive, paranoid intensity — a raw, disturbing document that blurs the line between confession, accusation, and breakdown.

title: Inferno
author: August Strindberg
description: Strindberg's account of his catastrophic mental crisis of the 1890s — hallucinations, persecution fears, alchemical experiments, and a fragile spiritual recovery — one of the most harrowing memoirs of a creative breakdown ever written.

title: The Father
author: August Strindberg
description: A cavalry captain is driven to madness and death by his wife's subtle campaign to control their daughter's upbringing, in Strindberg's first great naturalist tragedy — a ferocious study of power, gender, and psychological warfare.

title: Miss Julie
author: August Strindberg
description: On Midsummer Night, an aristocratic young woman and her father's valet play out a fatal erotic game of class and dominance that ends in irreversible destruction — the definitive one-act tragedy of the naturalist movement.

title: The Dance of Death
author: August Strindberg
description: A military officer and his wife are imprisoned together in a coastal fortress, locked in a decades-long war of mutual destruction — Strindberg's vision of marriage as prolonged combat, bleak and darkly comic.

title: A Dream Play
author: August Strindberg
description: The daughter of the god Indra descends to earth and passes through a kaleidoscope of human suffering, desire, and absurdity in Strindberg's breakthrough expressionist drama — a founding text of twentieth-century theatrical modernism.

title: The Ghost Sonata
author: August Strindberg
description: A young student is drawn into a dinner party whose respectable bourgeois guests conceal secrets, crimes, and a curse, in Strindberg's chamber play — a ghostly, symbolist masterwork that prefigures the Theatre of the Absurd.

title: Seven Gothic Tales
author: Karen Blixen
description: Seven ornate, aristocratic stories set in a timeless romantic Europe — full of disguises, shipwrecks, and storytelling within storytelling — that made Isak Dinesen an instant sensation when published first in English.

title: Out of Africa
author: Karen Blixen
description: Blixen's lyrical memoir of her seventeen years running a coffee farm in Kenya — an elegy for a landscape, a people, and a way of life, written with a storyteller's art and one of the most beautiful prose memoirs of the twentieth century.

title: Winter's Tales
author: Karen Blixen
description: A collection of twelve tales ranging from a Danish folktale to stories of artists, sailors, and noblewomen, unified by a sense of fate and the consoling power of narrative — Blixen's most varied and humane collection.

title: Last Tales
author: Karen Blixen
description: Blixen's penultimate collection, including several tales set in a great fictional monastery and others returning to Africa and Denmark, showing her gothic imagination at its most philosophically ambitious.

title: Anecdotes of Destiny
author: Karen Blixen
description: Five tales including the celebrated 'Babette's Feast' — in which a French refugee cook transforms a Norwegian pietist community through a single magnificent dinner — gathered in Blixen's most accessible and beloved collection.

title: Ehrengard
author: Karen Blixen
description: A court painter boasts he can seduce any woman and sets his sights on the chaste lady-in-waiting guarding a secret royal pregnancy, in Blixen's final, witty, and deceptively light novella about art, desire, and shame.

title: My Struggle: Book 1
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: Knausgård reconstructs the death of his alcoholic father and the humiliating weeks of clearing out his grandmother's house, launching his six-volume autobiographical novel with brutal honesty about grief, shame, and male inadequacy.

title: My Struggle: Book 2
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: Life as a young father and writer in Stockholm — the chaos of small children, the longing for solitude, the hunger for art — rendered in obsessive, unguarded detail that made this the most electrifying volume of the series for many readers.

title: My Struggle: Book 3
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: Knausgård's childhood on a Norwegian island in the 1970s — school, football, pop music, and the ever-present dread of his cold, controlling father — reconstructed with astonishing sensory precision and no sentimentality.

title: My Struggle: Book 4
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: Eighteen-year-old Knausgård takes a teaching post in remote northern Norway, drinks, chases girls, and yearns to become a writer, in the most picaresque and darkly comic volume of the series.

title: My Struggle: Book 5
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: The years at the Bergen Writing Academy, a disastrous first marriage, paralyzing creative failure, and the slow emergence of the writer he will become — Knausgård's most artistically self-conscious volume.

title: My Struggle: Book 6
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: The monumental finale interweaves a lengthy essay on Hitler and Mein Kampf with the fallout from publishing the series itself — family fury, media storm, and a reckoning with what it means to sacrifice everything for literature.

title: A Time for Everything
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: An audacious fictional meditation on the Book of Job and the nature of angels, spanning centuries from Noah's flood to the modern age — Knausgård's most ambitious pre-Struggle novel, blending theology, natural history, and lyric prose.

title: The Morning Star
author: Karl Ove Knausgård
description: Over a single late-summer day and night in Norway, a bright new star appears in the sky and several unconnected people's lives begin to shift toward darkness and the supernatural, in Knausgård's sweeping return to fiction.

title: R.U.R.
author: Karel Čapek
description: The play that gave the world the word 'robot': artificial workers manufactured to free humanity from labor rise up and annihilate their creators, in Čapek's prophetic science fiction drama about technology, exploitation, and what it means to be human.

title: War with the Newts
author: Karel Čapek
description: An intelligent aquatic species is discovered, exploited as cheap labor by capitalist civilization, and methodically educated — until the Newts begin sinking the continents in Čapek's devastating satirical allegory of fascism and human self-destruction.

title: The Insect Play
author: Karel Čapek
description: A tramp watches insects enact human vices — the butterflies' frivolous lust, the beetles' acquisitive greed, the ants' militarist totalitarianism — in the darkly comic satirical play Čapek wrote with his brother Josef.

title: The Makropulos Affair
author: Karel Čapek
description: A seemingly ageless opera singer holds the key to an ancient inheritance dispute, and eventually reveals she is over three hundred years old — Čapek's philosophical play about whether immortality would be a gift or a curse.

title: The White Disease
author: Karel Čapek
description: A mysterious plague that kills only the middle-aged spreads across Europe while a dictator prepares for war, and only a pacifist doctor possesses the cure — Čapek's urgent anti-fascist drama written on the eve of the Nazi threat.

title: The Absolute at Large
author: Karel Čapek
description: A machine that burns matter perfectly also releases God as a byproduct, causing mass religious ecstasy and then global holy war — Čapek's satirical novel about the catastrophic consequences of both technology and mass faith.

title: Krakatit
author: Karel Čapek
description: A feverish engineer invents an explosive of terrifying power and, delirious with illness, races across Central Europe while governments and conspirators try to steal his formula — a prophetic thriller about the ethics of scientific discovery.

title: An Ordinary Life
author: Karel Čapek
description: A retired railway clerk writes his life story and discovers it contains not one self but many — the third of Čapek's philosophical trilogy demonstrating that a single human life resists definitive interpretation.

title: Apocryphal Tales
author: Karel Čapek
description: Short, witty retellings of biblical stories, classical myths, and historical episodes from unexpected angles — Pilate defending himself, Hamlet explaining his hesitation — showcasing Čapek's philosophical playfulness and ironic humanism.

title: Love in a Fallen City
author: Eileen Chang
description: Novellas set in 1940s Shanghai and Hong Kong capturing the erotic negotiations of women navigating a decaying world — written when Chang was barely twenty-three and immediately establishing her as a defining voice of modern Chinese literature.

title: Lust, Caution
author: Eileen Chang
description: A young actress in the Chinese resistance movement is tasked with seducing and assassinating a collaborator, but finds her own desire fatally complicating the mission — Chang's most celebrated and morally ambiguous novella.

title: Half a Lifelong Romance
author: Eileen Chang
description: A modest Shanghai factory worker and a gentle engineer fall in love but are separated by family scheming and the upheavals of history, reuniting decades later in Chang's most emotionally devastating novel.

title: The Rice Sprout Song
author: Eileen Chang
description: A young woman returns from Shanghai to her rural village during collectivization and watches her family destroyed by ideology and famine — Chang's taut, unsentimental portrayal of Communist land reform, written in English.

title: Little Reunions
author: Eileen Chang
description: Chang's most autobiographical novel, following a gifted young woman from wartime Shanghai through her affair with a collaborationist intellectual — withheld for decades by the author herself and finally published posthumously.

title: Naked Earth
author: Eileen Chang
description: A young Shanghai engineer swept up in the Korean War and subsequent political campaigns finds his loyalties, identity, and survival instincts tested at every turn in Chang's English-language anti-totalitarian novel.
